title: Lec-48: What is Classless Addressing (CIDR) in Hindi | CIDR vs Classful Addressing

segments.text: Hello friends, welcome to Gate Smashers. The topic is Classless Addressing.
The concept of Classless Addressing came in 1993 after the concept of Classful Addressing.
In Classful Addressing we divide the IP Addresses into 5 classes.
Because of that there is a problem of flexibility and what other problems
or we can say disadvantages occur in Classful Addressing, for that you can follow my video
and in this video we are going to discuss only the concept of Classless Addressing.
Let's start Classless Addressing. First of all, first point is no classes,
means here you forget about Class A, B, C, D. There is no funda of classes here.
Here the concept is of blocks. Block means, if user says to me
that I want x amount of IP Addresses, then I provide him x amount of IP Addresses.
Means whatever is the user's demand, that size of block I provide the user.
But in Classful Addressing, there's nothing for the user, user says that I want
let's say 1000 IP Addresses, so there is no funda of 1000 IP Addresses there.
Maybe, if I give a Class A address, in Class A there are 1 crore Addresses in my network.
So user has demanded only 1000, but I can't fulfil his demand as it is
if I am following Classful Addressing. So in Classless Addressing,
wastage of IP Addresses is very less because whatever is the user's demand,
a block of that size, block means how many IP Addresses he wants,
I will provide him with only those many IP Addresses, because it is handled by IENA
Internet Assigned Number Authority. The block size that user demands will be provided to him.
Now block means what here? One is the Block ID and another is the Host ID.
Means there we had Network ID and Host ID, here we are talking about a block.
So block address here talking about IPv4, so in IPv4, IP Address is of 32 bits,
but now the MSC bits in it represent what? Block ID. Means the block,
we are talking about the network, you can take network instead of block,
but it is not fixed as in Classful. And other bits represent what?
How many hosts are there in that block, that is what it is representing,
we will see with the example. Next is the notation, this is a very important point.
Notation in Classless Addressing is x.y.z.w means the same, I have 4 octets,
1 octet for x, y, z and w. But the new thing is /n. What does /n notation mean here?
here n represents mask, you can call this mask, or
number of bits represent block or network.
What is the funda of n here? Otherwise if an IP Address is given to me, how will I know
how many hosts are there? Or what is the Network ID? So to tell me this /n will help,
because here n is working as a mask. Mask means continuous 1s.
What is the number of 1s is represented by /n. Which we can say in other terms,
number of bits used to represent the Network ID part.
Network ID part or Block ID part, that is what is n. We'll see an example,
Now you know that this is a normal IP Address which you have seen before.
What is a new thing here? /28. Now what is the concept of /28 here?
There are 28 number of 1s. Means
Network ID part is represented by 28 number of bits. So how much is the Host ID part?
If I say this is a 32 bit IP Address, then what is this 28? This.
So what is remaining? 32 minus 28 that is 4. Because 28 and 4 is how much? 32.
So here the first question you can be asked, because no one will ask what is the class,
like it was there that it belongs to C Class, there is no classes concept here,
no one will ask what is the class of this IP Address.
Here the first question you can be asked is this IP Address belongs to which network
and how many hosts are in that network? So you take this /28 first.
28 means what? Number of continuous 1s, means part of Network ID.
So part of Network ID is of how many bits? 28 bits.
So what is part of Host ID? 32-28 that is 4.
So number of bits used to represent the Host, 4. So what is the number of hosts?
2 raised to power 4 that is 16. So here my first question that comes,
what is the number of hosts in this network? That is 16.
Next part is that this is an IP Address. IP Address means what?
There is a network of some ABC organization, in that ABC organization
one IP Address is this one. This one with 20.40... But
what is the Network ID of that ABC? Means the whole world doesn't know it by this ID,
this is one of the IP Addresses in that network, that is 10.20.40
But to find what is the ID of this network, we did what? We did AND with the mask.
But here, how did we find mask in Classful? Each class has its own mask.
Like Class A has 255.0.0.0, Class B has 255.255.0.0
This I have mentioned in old videos of Classes A, B, C.
Here I don't know mask but I have already told you what is this n? It is the mask,
means number of 1s. You can write it as 11111111.11111111.11111111.11110000
Why I wrote it like this because how many number of 1s are there? 28.
You convert this into mask, you already know what is continuous eight 1s,
These three become 255.255.255 Now what is this?
What is this representing here? 128, then it is 192, then 224, then 240.
Means here your mask becomes what? This becomes your mask that is
This is the mask of this network. Now here you have to
find the Network ID of this IP Address, which network this IP Address belongs to.
So I am telling you a simple concept, that in this network, in this IP Address
you have to start 28 bits from MSV, means you have to start from the very left,
don't even touch the 28 bits, keep 28 bits as it is. 28 bits means what?
How many bits in 200? Means this represents 8 bits, this represents 8 bits,
this represents 8 bits. 8*2 is 16, 8*3 is 24. Means 24 bits are here.
Open only 40, means represent it in binary. If I represent 40 in binary,
then what will come? 00101000
2 raised to power 0, 1, 2, 3, that is 8.
Now what do you have to do? I am telling you a simple method, 28 bit you know that
28 bit is part of network and we have already discussed never to change network ID
because if Network ID is changed, then your organization will change means
your entire network is changed. So don't touch the network bits
and how many bits are there? 28. So these 8 bits, 16, 24, now open this remaining 40
because 4 bits we have to pick here. So 8 bits here, 16 bits here, 24 here,
this comes upto 28, means you take it upto 0010.
Now this is total how many bits? 28. Means these bits you don't have to touch.
Remaining are how much? These 4 bits remain. And you already know host bits are 4.
So means these 4 bits will only remain for host bits. Now you make these host bits 0
means make all host bits 0. Means I will convert this into 0000.
Write others as it is, only convert this and write the octet before this as it is.
What is the octet before? 0010. Means wrote above bits as it is 0010,
and converted these 4 bits into 0000. Due to this what will happen?
You will find out the network of this IP Address. So just write 200 as it is,
write 10 as it is, write 20 as it is, but this remaining portion becomes how much?
0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. 2 raised to power 5 is 32.
So yes, I got the Network ID of, or Block ID of this IP address.
And what is the Block ID? 200.10.20.32, don't forget to put /n which is /28
So here a very important point I come to know that
the network, which this IP Address belongs to, the whole world knows it by which name?
So means I have found out the network of this IP Address.
If you want to follow the second method, that is also simple, similar type of method.
You found out default mask. With this mask you AND this one, this IP Address.
If I do AND let's say, mask we found out, we wrote 28 as it is.
We wrote 28 bits, continuous 1s, as this which I just said. 10.20.40
You perform AND operation on this, and we have done AND multiple times,
if you AND any value with 255, means with all 1s, result will be the same value.
You don't have to do anything, copy it as it is.
If you want to do, 255 is all 1s, below if you open 200,
you will already have 1 at the same place because 1-1 is already 1,
so your value will always be the same. But this is not 255, this is 240.
And what is 40? If you convert 40, then 40 will be 00101000
Yes, so this is 40. Now perform AND operation on it, 1-0 0, 1-0 0,
And if you convert it into decimal, it will come out to be 32.
So you either do AND with this or remember as it is that don't touch these bits,
you simply convert host bits into 0,
you can follow any of the 2 methods, your answer will be the same.
So this is why CIDR, Classless Addressing concept is very important
and many times question comes in exam on this. The first question you may get that
you have to find out that the network that this IP belongs to
what is the number of hosts in it? We found that out.
And which block or network this IP Address belongs to, that also we found out.
Last point here I will tell you rules, here are the rules for Classless Addressing.
First rule is, Addresses should be contiguous. Means all the addresses in that block
should be contiguous. Second, number of addresses in a block must be power of 2.
Addresses should be contiguous means what? Like if address is 200.10.20.32
then after that 33, 34, 35, 36, this way they should be contiguous.
In between it should not interleave means nothing should skip in between.
If it is there, then according to CIDR it is not valid.
Second, number of addresses must be power of 2. Number of addresses means
in this block, like if I talk about this, how many hosts are in this block? 16.
Means how many number of IP Addresses we can generate in this network? 16.
And 16 value is in power of 2, 2 raised to power 4 is 16.
So yes, second point is also valid according to that. If 2 raised to power,
means if I say that number of addresses in a network is 17,
so 17 is not a power of 2, so this will be invalid. Third point is,
first address of every block must be evenly divisible by size of the block.
Means whatever network you are talking about, its first address,
and what will be the first address? The first address will show the network.
So the ID of that network should be divisible by what? Size of the block.
Now here if you see, how can you do it, I'm telling a simple method.
You have found this out. This is our first address. This 40 one that came,
here 32, this is the first address of this network because it is the Network ID.
If you divide this, what is the size of the block? Size of the block is 16.
So you don't have to divide as it is with 16, to divide it what you have to do is
a simple method, the last octet means this 16 value is 2 raised to power 4,
so these 4 bits here should be 0, means the last 4 LSB bits,
Least Significant Bits which are 4, should be what? 00. Means if you open 32,
then what will it be? 32 is 00100000
Now here what is the size of the block? Block size is 2 raised to power 4.
So to do this you don't have to divide by 16, 2 raised to power 4 means
you simply take these 4 bits, the last 4 bits, 1, 2, 3, 4.
That is the LSB, so if last 4 bits are 0, that means it is evenly divisible.
If this IP Address was 33, then in case of 33 what would come here?
That's what 33 would be, and if you divide 33 then are the last 4 bits 0? No.
Last 4 bits are not coming as 0, so this is how you can find out
whether the first address is divisible by size of the block or not.
So this is all about the CIDR that is Classless Inter Domain Routing.
Thank you.
